title: Needle-felted abstract brooch
subtitle: A small freeform disc in layered merino colours
excerpt: Build a compact disc or oval from layered merino roving using a needle, then attach a brooch back. The abstract form works in two or three contrasting colours with no fixed design; the result is a wearable piece with a painterly, layered look.
sourceNotes: Synthesised from contemporary British needle-felting wearables practice. Abstract flat brooches are a well-established quick project in UK felting workshops.

glossaryTerms.definition: A dry sculpting method using barbed needles to lock wool fibres together without water. Repeated needle stabs force the barbs through the fibres, tangling and binding them into a dense, stable shape.

content.text: An abstract brooch is a fast, satisfying piece of

. A small disc of merino is built up, then a second and third colour are laid in patches on the surface and needled to create a layered, painterly result. No two will look exactly alike. The disc can be any shape: a perfect circle, an irregular oval, or a curved square.

attrs.title: Needle safety
attrs.body: Stab straight in and out at 90 degrees onto the foam pad only. Always use a finger guard on the supporting hand.

content.text: Pull a few wisps of the base colour merino and layer them in a loose circle about 5 cm across on the mat. Keep the layers thin. Add three or four layers, rotating 45 degrees with each layer so the fibres run in all directions. Needle the whole stack firmly with the size-38 needle until it begins to hold together as one piece. The disc should be about 4 mm thick after this stage.

Work the needle around the outer edge, angling it slightly inward to tuck any stray fibres into the disc. This creates a clean edge. Alternatively, leave a slightly irregular edge for a more natural look. Needle both the front and back surfaces to produce a flat, even disc.

Lay small wisps of the second colour across parts of the disc surface. Do not cover the whole base; aim for overlapping patches that let the base colour show through in places. Needle the second colour in firmly with the size-40 needle, keeping the strokes shallow so the colour sits on the surface rather than sinking through to the back. Add the third colour in smaller patches on top of the second, creating a layered depth.

Go over the whole front surface once more with the size-40 needle in light, even passes to smooth the colour transitions. The back of the disc should remain plain base colour with a smooth, flat texture. Attach the brooch back by stitching it to the plain back surface with a few passes of strong thread through the felt.

items.problem: The colour layers have sunk through to the back of the disc
items.cause: The needle strokes were too deep or too many passes were made
items.fix: Keep the needle angle more vertical and limit passes over the colour areas to three or four per section. The colours should sit near the surface; deep felting obscures them.

items.problem: The disc has bent or warped as it dried
items.cause: The base disc was not felted evenly on both sides
items.fix: Place the disc face-down on a flat surface and press firmly. Lightly dampen the back if necessary; felt is easy to reshape when slightly damp.
